{{< include /_macros.qmd >}}

# Self-Attention from a Graph Viewpoint {#sec-attention-as-gnn}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## In this chapter
<!-- The book's central pedagogical move: justify self-attention on its own
     terms as message passing on a fully connected graph — no RNN history
     required. -->
:::

**Learning objectives.**

<!-- TODO -->
- [ ]
- [ ]

## Sequences as sets, tokens as nodes

<!-- A sequence is a set of tokens (plus positions, later). What operations
     on sets are permutation-equivariant and why we want that. -->

## Message passing

<!-- The generic GNN update: aggregate neighbours, then update. Sum/mean
     aggregation and its limits. -->

## Learned, content-based edges

<!-- The key idea: instead of a fixed graph, let each node choose whom to
     listen to via a learned, data-dependent weighting. This *is* attention. -->

## Why this framing pays off

<!-- Sets up multi-head as multiple relation types, causal masking as a
     directed graph, positional encoding as node features. -->

## Exercises {.unnumbered}

<!-- TODO -->

## Further reading {.unnumbered}
